title: NAT egress
text: Private subnets reach the internet through NAT gateways, one per availability zone, so tasks can pull images and call Bedrock without being publicly reachable.

title: Subnet CIDRs
text: The VPC uses a /16 CIDR block carved into /20 subnets, leaving headroom to add more services without renumbering the network.

title: VPC endpoints
text: Gateway and interface VPC endpoints keep S3 and ECR traffic on the AWS backbone, avoiding NAT charges and reducing exposure for the vector store on S3.

title: Availability zones
text: Spreading subnets across three zones lets the load balancer and ECS service survive the loss of a single zone without downtime.

title: Application Load Balancer
text: The Application Load Balancer terminates TLS and forwards requests to the retrieval-api target group on the container port.

title: Listener rules
text: Listener rules route by path: a single condition block matches the /search prefix and forwards to the retrieval-api target group.

title: Target group health
text: The target group health check hits /health; unhealthy tasks are drained and replaced before they receive traffic.

title: HTTPS redirect
text: An HTTP listener redirects every request to HTTPS so no query is ever served over plaintext.

title: Deregistration delay
text: A short deregistration delay lets in-flight searches finish during a deploy before a task is removed from the target group.

title: ECS Fargate service
text: retrieval-api runs as an ECS Fargate service so there are no EC2 hosts to patch; the platform manages the underlying capacity.

title: Task definition
text: The task definition pins CPU and memory, the container image tag, and the environment variables that select the embedder and reranker forks.

title: Rolling deploys
text: The service uses a rolling deployment with a minimum healthy percent so a new revision is validated by health checks before the old one is stopped.

title: Autoscaling
text: Target-tracking autoscaling adds tasks when average CPU or request count per target crosses a threshold, keeping p99 latency inside the SLO under load.

title: Task role
text: Each task assumes an IAM task role scoped to exactly the S3 prefix and Bedrock models it needs, nothing more.

title: Firn on S3
text: Firn stores vectors and the full-text index as objects on S3, so the corpus is durable and cheap and needs no database server to operate.

title: Foyer cache
text: Firn's foyer cache keeps hot vectors and postings in memory and on local disk; the cache-hit rate is a property of Firn, not a separate cache tier.

title: No ElastiCache
text: There is deliberately no ElastiCache or external cache in front of retrieval; caching lives inside Firn so the hit-rate story is not bolted on.

title: Corpus objects
text: Documents, their embeddings, and the inverted index are written as immutable objects; a new corpus version is a new set of objects, never an in-place edit.

title: Cold reads
text: On a cache miss Firn reads segments from S3 and warms the foyer cache, so the first query after a deploy is slower than the steady state.

title: Query embedding
text: Each incoming query is embedded into a dense vector before the vector search runs; the same model embeds the documents at index time.

title: Bedrock embeddings
text: The embedder can call Amazon Bedrock; the model id is configuration, so switching models never requires a code change.

title: Self-hosted embeddings
text: Embedding can move onto a GPU EC2 instance behind the same embedder interface, trading per-call cost for cold-start complexity.

title: Embedder as a fork
text: Bedrock versus self-hosted inference is a deliberate fork kept addressable by an environment variable rather than decided prematurely.

title: Embedding dimensionality
text: Higher-dimensional embeddings capture more nuance but cost more memory in the foyer cache; the dimension is tuned against the eval gate.

title: Rerank pass
text: After hybrid retrieval, an optional rerank pass reorders the top candidates with a stronger cross-encoder before the response is returned.

title: Bedrock reranker
text: The reranker can be a Bedrock model; like the embedder it is selected by configuration so the fork stays open.

title: Rerank window
text: Reranking runs only over a small candidate window from the first-stage retrieval, keeping the extra latency bounded.

title: Rerank and latency
text: Because reranking adds a model call it is measured against the p99 SLO; it is only enabled when the quality gain justifies the latency.

title: Identity reranker
text: Until the real reranker is wired, an identity pass preserves ordering so the rerank code path and its span are exercised end to end.

title: Eval gate purpose
text: The eval gate refuses to promote a build if retrieval quality or latency regresses; infra is up-or-down, but retrieval can be up and returning garbage.

title: Recall at k
text: The gate measures recall@10 against a versioned golden set: the fraction of known-relevant documents that appear in the top ten results.

title: Thresholds and baseline
text: Thresholds set an absolute recall floor and a maximum allowed drop versus a committed baseline; the baseline only moves through a deliberate pull request.

title: Golden set
text: The golden set is versioned in the repository, one query and its relevant ids per line, and grows like a test suite through reviewed changes.

title: Failing the build
text: The harness writes eval-report.json and exits non-zero on any breach, so the promote stage cannot run when quality or latency has regressed.

title: p99 latency SLO
text: The gate enforces a p99 latency SLO measured from a fixed, repeatable load run against a warmed staging endpoint.

title: Warm versus cold
text: Latency is measured warm, after the foyer cache is primed, so the number reflects steady-state serving rather than cold starts.

title: Load run
text: A fixed load generator replays a set of queries at a target concurrency and records the latency distribution used for the SLO check.

title: Tail latency
text: Tail latency matters more than the average because a slow p99 means a real fraction of users wait too long, even if the mean looks fine.

title: Latency as a metric
text: The p99 result is emitted as a metric so latency is graphable over time in Grafana, not just a pass or fail in one pipeline run.

title: OpenTelemetry spans
text: Every operation that crosses a process boundary emits an OpenTelemetry span; the retrieval-api instruments each handler and each downstream call.

title: OTel collector
text: The service exports spans over OTLP to an OpenTelemetry Collector, which fans them out to the chosen trace backend.

title: Trace backend fork
text: Tempo versus FirnTel is the second deliberate fork; the collector's exporter is swapped without touching application code.

title: Span attributes
text: Spans carry attributes like the query length, k, and result count, so a slow trace can be read without guessing what the request was.

title: OTel versus Prometheus
text: OpenTelemetry is how telemetry leaves the app; Prometheus and Grafana are where metrics land — layers, not competitors.

title: Prometheus metrics
text: The collector forwards metrics to Prometheus, which stores the time series that Grafana then visualises.

title: Grafana dashboards
text: Grafana dashboards show request rate, error rate, p99 latency, and the eval-gate recall over time on one pane.

title: Eval results as metrics
text: Eval-gate results are emitted as metrics, so retrieval quality is a graph that trends, not only a pass or fail buried in CI logs.

title: Alerting
text: Alerts fire when p99 crosses the SLO or recall drops below the floor, catching regressions that slip past a single pipeline run.

title: RED method
text: Dashboards follow the RED method — rate, errors, duration — so an on-call engineer can triage the request path at a glance.

title: Pipeline stages
text: The pipeline validates and formats, scans policy, builds the image, applies to staging, runs the eval gate, and only then promotes to prod.

title: Policy scanning
text: Static policy scanning with checkov and trivy is an infra gate; a pipeline that skips it is considered broken.

title: Promotion
text: Promotion to prod is guarded by the eval gate: the promote job runs only if the recall and latency checks pass.

title: Remote state
text: Terraform state lives in S3 with locking; every change goes through plan output in the pipeline rather than an ad-hoc console edit.

title: Bad corpus demo
text: A branch with a deliberately bad corpus fails the eval stage on purpose, proving the gate can stop a low-quality build from shipping.

title: Least privilege
text: Every role follows least privilege: the task role can read only its S3 prefix and invoke only the Bedrock models it actually uses.

title: CI role
text: The pipeline assumes a scoped deploy role via OIDC, so no long-lived cloud keys are stored in the CI system.

title: Secrets handling
text: Configuration is environment-driven and secrets come from the parameter store at task start, never baked into the image.

title: Network isolation
text: Security groups allow only the ALB to reach the task port, and only the tasks to reach S3 and Bedrock endpoints.

title: Audit trail
text: All infrastructure changes flow through reviewed plans, giving a complete audit trail with no out-of-band console edits.

title: VPC layout
text: The reference VPC spans three availability zones with public and private subnets in each. Public subnets hold the ALB; private subnets hold the Fargate tasks.
